retrieved_docs.title: Parotidectomy -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Trismus: Inflammation of the master muscle is transient and mild.

retrieved_docs.title: Parotidectomy -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The following are common complications after parotidectomy:

retrieved_docs.title: Parotidectomy -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: First-bite syndrome: Painful spasm in the parotid region occurs at the first bite during mastication and decreases afterward. This pain accompanies each meal usually. The syndrome is a unique complication of surgeries targeting the deep lobe of the parotid gland, the parapharyngeal space, and the infratemporal fossa. The presumed etiology is the loss of sympathetic innervation of the parotid gland, leading to relative parasympathetic overstimulation, resulting in the contraction of parotid myoepithelial cells. Leaving the superficial lobe in place increases the risk of first-bite syndrome. Treatment is often challenging and begins with carbamazepine, which must be titrated. Most patients report improvement with time but never complete recovery. This is exceedingly rare and only encountered in extensive parotidectomy and extended neck dissections that violate the deep fascia of the floor of the neck, traumatizing the superior cervical ganglion. Loss of sensation around the ear, especially the lobule, occurs frequently. Significant but incomplete improvement is expected within 1 year. Preservation of the posterior branch of the great auricular nerve will hasten recovery. Amputation neuroma might occur when the nerve is transected; treatment is simply excision. Surgical site depression occurs proportionally to the amount of tissue resected. Reconstruction with an SCM flap is helpful, but the drawback of creating a defect in the neck should be considered. An autologous fat graft can also be used.

retrieved_docs.title: Malignant Salivary Gland Tumors -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: The incidence of temporary facial nerve palsy after parotidectomy ranges from 10% to 65%, with permanent paralysis seen in less than 5%. [146] [220] [221] The incidence of Frey syndrome after parotidectomy varies widely from 2% to 80% due to the time interval since surgery as well as the surveillance criteria from the surgeons. [222] Treatment for Frey syndrome includes antiperspirant ointment, botulinum toxin A injections, and barrier flaps such as superficial musculoaponeurotic system flap, temporoparietal flap, sternocleidomastoid flap, anterolateral thigh flap, or thick skin flaps. [222] [223] [224] [225] Additional complications from surgical resection include sialocele, salivary fistula, neuromas of the great auricular nerve, and preauricular skin anesthesia. [113] Patients who undergo submandibular gland resection or neck dissection for cervical lymph node metastasis have a risk of neurological damage to the spinal accessory, phrenic, hypoglossal, lingual, vagus, sympathetic trunk, and the marginal mandibular branch of the facial nerve. [226] [227] [228] Complications from radiation include sensorineural hearing loss, chronic otitis media/externa, otalgia, skin erythema, mucositis, dysphagia, dysgeusia, xerostomia, soft tissue fibrosis, osteoradionecrosis, and radiation-induced malignancy. [34] [113] In a study, approximately 36% of patients were found to develop hearing loss of 10 dB or higher at 4kHz. [229] Mandibular osteoradionecrosis (<2%) and radiation-induced malignancy (1%) at 10 to 25 years are rare complications. [113] [230] [231] [232]

retrieved_docs.title: Benign Salivary Gland Tumors -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical excision is the standard treatment for all salivary gland tumors. This may lead to a loss of functional tissue and saliva secretion, causing xerostomia, dental caries, halitosis, periodontal disease, and oral infections. [107] The incidence of temporary facial nerve palsy after parotidectomy ranges from 10% to 65%, with permanent paralysis seen in less than 5%. [128] [129] [130] The incidence of Frey syndrome after parotidectomy varies widely from 2% to 80% due to the time interval since surgery as well as the surveillance criteria from the surgeons. [131] Treatments for Frey syndrome have included antiperspirant ointment, botulinum toxin A injections, and barrier flaps such as superficial musculoaponeurotic system (SMAS) flap, temporoparietal flap, sternocleidomastoid flap, anterolateral thigh flap, or thick skin flap. [131] [132] [133] [134] Additional complications from surgical resection include sialocele, salivary fistula, neuromas of the great auricular nerve, and preauricular skin anesthesia. [51] Patients who undergo submandibular gland resection have a risk of neurological damage to the hypoglossal nerve, lingual nerve, and marginal mandibular branch of the facial nerve. [7] [135] [7] Complications from radiation include sensorineural hearing loss, chronic otitis media/externa, otalgia, skin erythema, mucositis, dysphagia, dysgeusia, xerostomia, soft tissue fibrosis, osteoradionecrosis, and radiation-induced malignancy. [94] [136] Approximately 36% of patients developed hearing loss of 10 dB and higher at 4kHz. [137] Mandibular osteoradionecrosis (<2%) and RT-induced malignancy (1%) at 10 to 25 years are rare complications. [136] [138] [139] [140]

retrieved_docs.title: Parotidectomy -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Seroma: This is managed by needle aspiration and compression dressing

retrieved_docs.title: Parotidectomy -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Frey syndrome: This syndrome is more accurately referred to as gustatory sweating. Patients report facial swelling and sweating at the site of the parotidectomy in occurrence with meals. The etiology is believed to be aberrant innervation of the sweat glands, with branches emerging from the auriculotemporal nerve after their division during surgery. This provides parasympathetic innervation to the normally sympathetic-innervated sweat glands. Diagnosis is usually based on patient history; however, if the iodine-starch test (Minor test) is inconclusive, the iodine starch placed on the affected area turns blue, signaling sweat secretion. The incidence historically has been reported as high as 50% to 100%, though, with modern techniques and the use of SMAS flaps and thicker skin flaps at the time of initial elevation, this is greatly reduced and is now quite rare. Should this syndrome develop, surgical treatment options are disappointing, with the best results obtained using SMAS and superficial temporal artery flaps as a barrier between the surgical site and the skin. The gold standard treatment now is botulinum toxin injection. Relief of symptoms is obtained for 6 to 36 months. This treatment works at the pre-synaptic level of the neuromuscular and neuroglandular junction by blocking the release of acetylcholine.

retrieved_docs.title: Frey Syndrome -- Etiology
retrieved_docs.content: Salivary gland neoplasms comprise 3-10% of all head and neck tumors and up to 86% of tumors located in the parotid gland. [10] [11] [12] Parotidectomy is the first-line treatment in patients with salivary gland neoplasms and the most common cause of Frey syndrome in adults. [13] [14] [15] Other complications after parotidectomy include facial paralysis, face contour asymmetry, scarring, salivary fistula, first bite syndrome, pain and discomfort, xerostomia, and hypoesthesia of the skin innervated by the great auricular nerve. [16] [17] [18] Larger parotid tumors are associated with more extensive dissection and parasympathetic nerve fiber exposure and damage, resulting in a higher risk for Frey syndrome. [19] Aside from parotidectomy, other causes of Frey syndrome include direct trauma to the parotid region, mandibular condyle fractures, temporomandibular joint fractures, infection, submandibular gland excision, and radical neck dissections. [5] [6] [9] [20] [21]

retrieved_docs.title: Parotidectomy -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: In the recovery room, facial nerve function should be assessed immediately. Some facial weakness is to be expected, particularly in total parotidectomy. This typically resolves with time so long as all branches are definitively identified and preserved, though full recovery can take many months. The drain is left in place until the output is less than 15 to 50 cc in 24 hours. The duration of compressive dressing is variable, and some experts remove the dressing when the drain can be removed; others insist on the importance of a compressive dressing, especially after drain removal.

retrieved_docs.title: Parotidectomy -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The patient needs to be counseled before surgery about the risk of temporary or permanent facial paralysis, the need for a suction drain, compressive dressing, and the possibility of an aesthetic contour deformity at the site of surgery. [26]

retrieved_docs.title: Parotidectomy -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: Parotid surgery is an emotionally intense experience for the patient and the family, with most fearing postoperative disfigurement. Fortunately, the results are frequently excellent for experienced surgeons. The clinician must explain clearly to the patient what to expect, tailoring such discussions to each patient. A small benign lesion may less likely result in permanent facial paralysis but is most dependent upon its location within the gland itself. An extensive malignant tumor poses a significant risk for facial nerve injury. An interdisciplinary approach is crucial to success.

retrieved_docs.title: Parotidectomy -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Medical treatment plays a minor role in the treatment of most parotid diseases. Therefore, surgical intervention remains the cornerstone of treatment. Parotidectomy is an intricate procedure with functional, aesthetic, and potentially oncologic considerations. Knowledge of pertinent anatomy, preoperative optimization, techniques for facial nerve identification, and complications will allow the surgeon to safely and effectively care for patients requiring parotidectomy.

retrieved_docs.title: Parotitis -- Complications -- Complications Related to Sialography and Surgical Interventions
retrieved_docs.content: Ductal perforation Vascular injury Complications related to anesthesia Postoperative ductal stenosis

retrieved_docs.title: Parotidectomy -- Complications
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical site infection: Prophylactic antibiotics are controversial. Extensive procedures with neck dissection and free flap reconstruction are at higher risk of infection and benefit from perioperative antibiotics. In more standard parotidectomy, intraoperative antibiotics are sufficient in most patients.

retrieved_docs.title: Malignant Salivary Gland Tumors -- Postoperative and Rehabilitation Care
retrieved_docs.content: Postoperative surveillance aims to detect locoregional recurrence and manage any treatment complications that may arise. Surgery and radiation can result in long-term swallowing difficulties (xerostomia, mucositis, trismus), as well as cosmetic defects from facial nerve paralysis. Early intervention for facial nerve reconstruction and rehabilitation and consultation with speech pathologists for swallowing difficulties is vital for allowing the patient to re-integrate as much as possible into their pre-treatment functional status and improve their quality of life. [226] [233] [234] Over 70% of recurrences occur within the first 3 years of treatment, except for low-grade malignancies and AdCC. According to the National Comprehensive Cancer Network (NCCN) guidelines for post-treatment surveillance, patients should receive regular follow up every 1 to 3 months in the 1 year after treatment, every 2-6 months in the 2 years, every 4-8 months in the 3 to 5 years, and every 12 months after the 5 years. [235] All salivary gland malignancies require post-treatment surveillance for up to 20 years (especially AdCC due to its propensity for delayed recurrences or metastases). Annual chest imaging should be performed for high-grade lesions and submandibular gland tumors due to the high risk of pulmonary metastasis. Thyroid hormones should be monitored every 6-12 months for neck radiation patients. [113]

retrieved_docs.title: Benign Salivary Gland Tumors -- Surgical Oncology
retrieved_docs.content: The disadvantages of superficial parotidectomy include excessive resection of parotid tissue leading to loss of parotid function, disruption of Stenson’s duct, facial contour defects, and facial nerve paralysis. Gland-preserving surgery has been recommended for benign tumors with a reduction in surgical complications and improvement in patient quality of life. [72] [85] [86] An alternative procedure, the partial superficial parotidectomy, has been proposed where the tumor is resected with a normal margin of parotid tissue, and the facial nerve is only dissected in the vicinity of the tumor. A comparison of the 2 techniques was studied by Roh et al who found better cosmesis, sensory, and salivary functions along with less facial nerve weakness and no difference in recurrence rate for partial superficial parotidectomy. [72]

Conversely, more extensive laminectomies involving multi‑segment exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina—without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy—are explicitly excluded from the excision category. Excision further extends to breast, urologic, and endocrine procedures. Nipple exploration with excision of the lactiferous duct, radical orchiectomy for tumor removal combined with abdominal exploration, and parathyroidectomy—whether performed with mediastinal exploration via a sternal split or transthoracic approach—are all described as excisional interventions. Beyond these, excision is linked to other therapeutic domains, serving as a method for procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it is associated with plastic procedures on the nose. It also encompasses percutaneous

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2576

Excision is also the method for “other or therapeutic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx,” and it is linked to plastic procedures on the nose, indicating a relationship between excisional techniques and nasal aesthetic surgery. Within the hierarchical structure of clinical actions, the group labeled “Clinical actions A‑G” is a child of Excision, making Excision the parent concept for these actions. Conversely, certain spinal operations are explicitly excluded from the Excision category: laminectomy with exploration or decompression of the spinal cord or cauda equina (without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy) at lumbar, sacral, or thoracic levels (one or two vertebral segments) are

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 4 -- Community 2756
retrieved_docs.content: Excision is presented as a broad surgical category that includes a wide variety of organ‑specific and region‑specific procedures while explicitly excluding certain extensive spinal decompressions. Within this category, adrenalectomy—whether partial or complete, performed transabdominally, lumbarly, or dorsally, and sometimes accompanied by removal of an adjacent retroperitoneal tumor—uses an excision method. Cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, either with cholangiography or with choledochoenterostomy, is also classified as an excision technique. Exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of the infratentorial (posterior fossa) region falls under the same umbrella. In the spine, excision encompasses laminotomy (hemilaminectomy) with nerve‑root decompression, partial facetectomy, foraminotomy, and removal of herniated intervertebral disc when performed at a single interspace in the cervical or lumbar spine. By contrast, more extensive laminectomy procedures that decompress the spinal cord or cauda equina over more than two vertebral segments—whether cervical, lumbar, or thoracic—are specifically listed as excluded methods for excision. Other procedures grouped under excision include nipple exploration with lactiferous‑duct excision, parathyroidectomy or parathyroid exploration, and parathyroidectomy that involves mediastinal exploration via a sternal split approach. Excision is positioned within the higher‑level grouping “Clinical actions A‑G,” indicating that it is one of the clinical actions encompassed by that classification. Additionally, excision is linked to plastic procedures on the nose and is identified as a method within the broader set of “other or therapeutic procedures on nose; mouth and pharynx,” reflecting its relevance to reconstructive and therapeutic interventions in those anatomical regions.

This framework supports precise anatomical localization, differential diagnosis, and therapeutic planning, especially the central role of nerve decompression procedures across multiple anatomical sites.

Together, Exploration provides an umbrella framework for targeted surgical investigations, while Excision represents the core operative technique that enables tissue removal, obstruction relief, diagnostic assessment and neural decompression, thereby optimizing patient outcomes across multiple specialties.

Specific procedures classified under the Excision method include partial or complete adrenalectomy with or without adrenal gland biopsy performed via transabdominal, lumbar, or dorsal routes; cholecystectomy combined with exploration of the common bile duct, with or without choledochoenterostomy; exploratory craniectomy or craniotomy of both infratentorial (posterior fossa) and supratentorial locations; suboccipital craniectomy aimed at cranial‑nerve exploration or decompression; radical orchiectomy for tumor removal that incorporates abdominal exploration; and parathyroidectomy procedures that involve mediastinal or cervical exploration, sometimes requiring a sternal split or transthoracic approach. In addition, Excision is linked to other therapeutic interventions on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, indicating that it

Recognizing this relationship clarifies the pathological nature and precise anatomical location of the disease, which is essential for accurate diagnosis, staging, and therapeutic planning in patients with salivary gland malignancies.

It is identified as the “method_of” other or therapeutic procedures on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, and it has a relationship with plastic procedures on the nose, suggesting that excisional techniques are employed both in therapeutic and aesthetic interventions of the nasal area. Overall, the knowledge base positions Excision as a central operative modality encompassing a wide range of minimally invasive and open surgical techniques across orthopedics, general surgery, neurosurgery, and endocrine surgery, while simultaneously defining its boundaries by excluding specific laminectomy approaches and connecting it to both therapeutic and plastic procedures of the facial region.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 933
retrieved_docs.content: The head and neck structure is identified as the anatomic site associated with cancer of the thyroid. Thyroid cancer is clinically associated with the procedure استئصال الدرقية (thyroidectomy). Thyroidectomy, whether performed as a partial or complete operation (thyroidectomy; partial or complete), is a targeted procedure for the head and neck structure and serves as the parent procedure for both استئصال الدرقية (full thyroidectomy) and استئصال الدرقية الجزئي (partial thyroidectomy). The partial thyroidectomy (استئصال الدرقية الجزئي) frequently co‑occurs with دراق, reflecting a contextual relationship. Understanding these interconnections is essential for planning appropriate surgical management of thyroid cancer within the head and neck region.

In addition, Excision is linked to other therapeutic interventions on the nose, mouth, and pharynx, indicating that it also serves as a methodological category for certain plastic or reconstructive surgeries in the facial region. Conversely, several laminectomy techniques—specifically decompressive laminectomy of the spinal cord or cauda equina without facetectomy, foraminotomy, or discectomy—are explicitly excluded from the Excision category. This exclusion applies to cervical, lumbar (except spondylolisthesis), sacral, and thoracic laminectomies involving one or two vertebral segments, as well as cervical laminectomies extending over more than two vertebral segments. Thus, Excision functions as an umbrella term for diverse operative procedures ranging from endocrine gland removal and biliary duct exploration to cranial and urogenital tumor resections, while clearly distinguishing itself from certain spinal
